category_signature.one_sentence_summary: A top-third CV for this category should present a senior IC or light player-coach who ships production AI platforms and features, proves architecture and reliability outcomes, and translates cross-functional needs into scalable applied AI systems rather than pure advanced experimentation or formal people management.
distinctive_signals: Architecture/platform scope is the clearest category anchor: platform design appears in 34/34 roles (100%), with most roles spanning both greenfield and optimization work (28/34).

This category is delivery-heavy and senior-IC-led: senior_ic appears in 34/34 roles, with scope split between IC (19/34) and player-coach (15/34), while hiring (3/34, 8.8%) and performance management (1/34, 2.9%) stay rare.

GenAI emphasis is practical rather than advanced experimentation-led: evaluation/quality is stronger (15/34, 44.1%) than RAG (10/34, 29.4%), agents/orchestration (9/34, 26.5%), prompt engineering (8/34, 23.5%), or fine-tuning (4/34, 11.8%).
citations: Seniority: senior_ic 34/34; role scope IC 19/34, player-coach 15/34.
Management signals: hiring 3/34 (8.8%), org building 8/34 (23.5%), performance management 1/34 (2.9%).
AI stack: evaluation_quality 15/34 (44.1%), RAG 10/34 (29.4%), agents_orchestration 9/34 (26.5%), prompt_engineering 8/34 (23.5%), fine_tuning 4/34 (11.8%).

headline_pattern.recommended_structure: <Staff|Principal> AI Engineer | Applied AI Platform & Architecture | Production GenAI, Evaluation, Reliability
evidence_first_rules: Use a Staff/Principal headline only when the CV already shows senior IC scope; title families cluster around staff_engineer (20/34) and principal_engineer (13/34).
Add an architecture/platform qualifier because platform design is universal in this sample (34/34, 100%).

Add GenAI or applied AI language only if the experience proves production delivery, evaluation, RAG, agents, or guardrails; no single technical keyword exceeds 60%, so keep the headline evidence-backed rather than tool-stuffed.

Do not imply formal people management, hiring ownership, or org leadership in the headline because those signals are limited: hiring 3/34 (8.8%), org building 8/34 (23.5%), performance management 1/34 (2.9%).
citations: Management signals: hiring 3/34 (8.8%), org building 8/34 (23.5%), performance management 1/34 (2.9%).
No hard skill crosses 60%; top hard skill Python is 11/34 (32.4%).

positioning_angle: Position as a hands-on architect for production AI systems who combines platform design, delivery ownership, and reliability evidence.
Show light technical leadership through mentoring, design influence, and stakeholder translation without overstating formal management.
foreground: Production architecture, system integration, reliability, evaluation quality, and scalable delivery across AI-enabled platforms.
Cross-functional execution and communication: collaboration 29/34 (85.3%), communication 23/34 (67.6%), mentoring 20/34 (58.8%).
Applied GenAI patterns when true: evaluation/quality 15/34 (44.1%), RAG 10/34 (29.4%), agents/orchestration 9/34 (26.5%), prompt engineering 8/34 (23.5%).
avoid: advanced experimentation-centric positioning, formal external credential language, or advanced academic-style framing.
Pure people-manager positioning or hiring-led narrative.
Niche stack laundry lists unsupported by outcomes.
safe_positioning: Staff/principal-level AI platform engineer who designs and productionizes scalable AI systems.
Senior IC or player-coach who improves reliability, latency, evaluation quality, and adoption of AI capabilities.
Architect-level engineer who bridges product, platform, and ML delivery in distributed environments.
unsafe_positioning: Formal org owner, people manager, or hiring lead unless explicitly evidenced.
Deep domain specialist in healthcare, fintech, observability, or consulting unless repeated work history proves it.
Cloud- or tooling-specific specialist claims such as deep Azure, Oracle, Grafana, Prometheus, or OpenTelemetry ownership unless explicitly evidenced.
citations: Unsafe claims sample includes formal people-management org ownership, strategic hiring ownership, deep healthcare ownership, deep consulting background, observability tool ownership, and deep oracle cloud specialization.

themes.theme: AI platform architecture and system design
themes.why_it_matters: This category consistently rewards engineers who can design reusable AI-enabled platforms and services, not just model experiments; platform design appears in 34/34 roles (100%).
themes.ats_guidance: Strongest keyword. Mention 'AI platform', 'architecture', or 'system design' 2-3 times across headline, profile, and experience if true.
themes.citation: Platform design 34/34 (100%); valued evidence type architecture 20/20 deep-analysis samples.

themes.why_it_matters: Top profiles show both building new systems and improving existing ones, which matches the dominant delivery pattern in the sample.
themes.ats_guidance: Secondary keyword. Mention 'greenfield', 'modernization', 'optimization', or equivalent 1-2 times if true.

themes.why_it_matters: The role sits closer to platform and production engineering than to isolated model work, so distributed systems, APIs, microservices, and integration depth can separate stronger CVs.
themes.ats_guidance: Secondary keyword cluster. Mention the strongest true terms 1-2 times; use niche infrastructure names once only if true.
themes.citation: Distributed systems 4/34 (11.8%), microservices 4/34 (11.8%), APIs 3/34 (8.8%); pain points emphasize robust data flows, serving APIs, large-scale maintainability, and low latency.

themes.why_it_matters: Reliability is repeatedly valued in deep analysis and in success metrics, so CV bullets should prove stable production operation rather than only feature shipment.
themes.ats_guidance: Strongest keyword. Mention 'reliability', 'resilience', 'high availability', or equivalent 2-3 times across profile and quantified bullets if true.
themes.citation: Valued evidence type reliability 18/20; success metrics include reliability, resilience, and high availability; monitoring appears in 4/34 (11.8%).

themes.theme: Evaluation and quality measurement for AI systems
themes.why_it_matters: Evaluation is the strongest AI-specific signal in this category and distinguishes production-minded AI engineers from generic GenAI implementers.
themes.ats_guidance: Strongest keyword. Mention 'evaluation', 'quality', 'benchmarking', or 'offline/online eval' 2-3 times if true.
themes.citation: Evaluation_quality 15/34 (44.1%); valued evidence type evaluation 15/20 deep-analysis samples.

themes.why_it_matters: Hiring pain points repeatedly mention low latency, high throughput, and maintainable scale, so outcome metrics here carry more weight than generic model claims.
themes.ats_guidance: Secondary keyword cluster. Mention latency, throughput, scale, cost, or operational excellence 1-2 times each when evidenced.
themes.citation: Valued evidence types scale 14/20 and cost 11/20; success metrics include scalability, performance, operational excellence, latency optimization; pain points cite low-latency and high-throughput constraints.

themes.why_it_matters: RAG is meaningful in this category but not universal, so it should strengthen the CV when real delivery evidence exists rather than dominate the narrative by default.
themes.ats_guidance: Strong secondary keyword. Mention 'RAG' or 'retrieval' 1-2 times if true; do not force it into every section.

themes.why_it_matters: Agentic workflows appear often enough to matter, especially when tied to production controls, reuse, or automation outcomes.
themes.ats_guidance: Secondary keyword. Mention 'agents', 'orchestration', or 'workflow automation' 1-2 times if true; keep specific frameworks once only.

themes.theme: Guardrails, prompting, and safe AI behavior
themes.why_it_matters: These signals support stronger applied AI stories, but they are still weaker than evaluation and platform delivery, so they should not anchor the whole CV unless deeply evidenced.
themes.ats_guidance: Niche keyword cluster. Mention guardrails, governance, prompt engineering, or routing once each and only if true.

themes.why_it_matters: This role requires translating architecture and delivery choices across product, platform, and engineering stakeholders; collaboration and communication are the most repeated soft-skill signals.
themes.ats_guidance: Strongest soft-skill keywords. Mention collaboration and communication 2-3 times across summary and leadership bullets if true.

themes.why_it_matters: Mentoring is common and supports a player-coach profile, but it should be framed as technical uplift rather than line management.
themes.ats_guidance: Secondary soft-skill keyword. Mention mentoring or coaching 1-2 times if true, ideally attached to code quality, architecture reviews, or team velocity outcomes.

themes.why_it_matters: Some roles include broader org-shaping influence, but the evidence does not support treating formal org ownership as standard for the category.
themes.ats_guidance: Niche phrasing only. Use 'influenced roadmap', 'aligned teams', or 'raised engineering standards' once if true; avoid manager language unless explicit.

key_achievement_archetypes.archetype: Built or evolved a production AI platform used by multiple teams or workflows
key_achievement_archetypes.what_it_proves: Shows the category's core value: platform architecture plus real delivery, reuse, and adoption at senior IC scope.
pain_points_addressed: build reusable ml infrastructure that supports training, deployment, and iteration
integrate ai/ml into product or platform stacks
keep systems maintainable, scalable, and low latency at large scale
metrics_to_include: number of teams or products onboarded
key_achievement_archetypes.story_format_guidance: Use ARIS: state the platform gap, the architecture you designed, the implementation scope you personally led, and quantified adoption/reliability outcomes. Keep the story centered on productionization, not abstract strategy.
key_achievement_archetypes.citation: Platform design 34/34 (100%); valued evidence types architecture 20/20, delivery 20/20, reliability 18/20, scale 14/20.

key_achievement_archetypes.archetype: Improved AI system quality with evaluation, benchmarking, or feedback loops
key_achievement_archetypes.what_it_proves: Demonstrates mature applied AI engineering and distinguishes the CV from generic GenAI delivery claims.
pain_points_addressed: make search, recommendation, or AI outputs more relevant
reduce regressions while iterating on AI features
metrics_to_include: quality lift, accuracy, precision/recall, or ranking gains
key_achievement_archetypes.story_format_guidance: Use STAR with a quality bottleneck as the tension point. Show the evaluation method you introduced, how it changed decisions or release safety, and the measurable quality or reliability gain.
key_achievement_archetypes.citation: Evaluation_quality 15/34 (44.1%); valued evidence type evaluation 15/20; success metrics include reliability, performance, and operational excellence.

key_achievement_archetypes.archetype: Shipped retrieval or agent workflows with safe operational behavior
key_achievement_archetypes.what_it_proves: Shows practical GenAI experience in the patterns most relevant to this category without drifting into advanced experimentation framing.
pain_points_addressed: use llms and agents to automate workflows
control failure modes, cost, and latency in AI services
metrics_to_include: cost per request or inference spend
key_achievement_archetypes.story_format_guidance: Use ARIS and explicitly name the workflow boundary, retrieval/agent design, and the controls that made it production-safe. Tie the result to cost, latency, quality, or adoption rather than novelty.
key_achievement_archetypes.citation: RAG 10/34 (29.4%), agents_orchestration 9/34 (26.5%), prompt_engineering 8/34 (23.5%), guardrails_governance 5/34 (14.7%), cost evidence 11/20.

key_achievement_archetypes.archetype: Scaled an AI-enabled service under latency, throughput, or reliability constraints
key_achievement_archetypes.what_it_proves: Signals that the candidate can handle the production engineering side of applied AI, which is central to staff/principal hiring in this sample.
pain_points_addressed: move models into production search or recommendation flows
keep systems maintainable and resilient at user scale
key_achievement_archetypes.story_format_guidance: Frame the story around an operational constraint. Show the architecture or service changes you drove and end with hard reliability, latency, or scale numbers.
key_achievement_archetypes.citation: Success metrics include scalability, performance, resilience, reliability, high availability, and latency optimization; valued evidence types reliability 18/20, scale 14/20, cost 11/20.

key_achievement_archetypes.archetype: Raised team delivery standards through technical leadership and mentoring
key_achievement_archetypes.what_it_proves: Supports a player-coach profile without overclaiming formal management and aligns with the category's strong collaboration and mentoring evidence.
pain_points_addressed: align cross-functional teams on architecture choices
improve execution quality across platform and product work
help teams adopt reusable AI patterns safely
metrics_to_include: adoption of standards, templates, or playbooks
key_achievement_archetypes.story_format_guidance: Use STAR with a delivery or quality coordination problem. Emphasize architecture reviews, standards, or mentoring actions and quantify the team-level effect without slipping into manager language.
key_achievement_archetypes.citation: Collaboration 29/34 (85.3%), communication 23/34 (67.6%), mentoring 20/34 (58.8%), player-coach scope 15/34, org_building 8/34 (23.5%).

highest_weight_roles: Staff/principal IC roles with platform, architecture, or applied AI delivery scope.
Technical lead or architect roles where the candidate designed production systems and influenced multiple teams.
Senior engineering roles with clear reliability, scale, API, distributed-systems, or MLOps-style outcomes that map into AI platform work.
expand_in_work_history: Roles with direct evidence of platform design, reusable infrastructure, production AI delivery, evaluation, reliability, or cross-team architecture influence.
Work that shows both greenfield building and optimization of existing systems.
Bullets with measurable outcomes in latency, uptime, quality, scalability, or adoption.
compress_in_work_history: Older roles that are primarily feature delivery without architecture scope.
Work centered on narrow tools or domains that do not map to platform, AI delivery, or production reliability.
People-management details unless they are explicit and material, because formal management is not the dominant pattern in this category.
how_to_frame_non_ai_experience: Translate non-AI platform work into transferable signals: distributed systems, APIs, microservices, cloud delivery, CI/CD, observability, reliability, and scale.
If the candidate has strong architecture experience outside AI, tie it forward to productionizing AI systems rather than pretending deep model specialization.
Use prior search, data, adtech, or backend scale work to prove relevance, ranking, routing, throughput, or serving depth when true.
citations: Valued evidence types architecture 20/20, delivery 20/20, reliability 18/20, scale 14/20.
Management signals remain limited: hiring 3/34 (8.8%), performance management 1/34 (2.9%).
Architecture/platform scope 34/34 (100%); management signals remain modest.

avoid_claims: Formal people-management org ownership unless explicitly evidenced; unsafe claim examples include formal people-management org ownership and direct people management or hiring ownership.
Strategic hiring ownership unless explicitly evidenced; hiring appears in only 3/34 roles (8.8%).
Deep healthcare, fintech, observability, consulting, or Oracle-specialist positioning unless repeated work history proves it.
Hands-on ownership of Grafana, Prometheus, Loki, Tempo, or OpenTelemetry in production unless the CV clearly shows it.
Primary ownership of front-end or unrelated stacks such as Ruby on Rails and React unless supported by actual experience.
End-to-end compliance, HIPAA, patient-safety, or responsible-AI program ownership unless explicitly stated.
buzzword_patterns: Do not stack 'LLMs, agents, RAG, guardrails, vector DBs, orchestration' without outcome evidence.
Do not use framework-heavy summaries when frameworks are individually sparse in the sample.
Do not present GenAI features as the whole job; keep architecture, delivery, reliability, and evaluation in front.
title_inflation_risks: Avoid upgrading senior engineer experience into staff/principal unless multi-team architecture and delivery scope is visible.
Avoid manager or director titles when the evidence supports senior IC or light player-coach positioning.
Avoid 'AI Research Lead' framing because research-heavy demand is only 1/34 (2.9%).
research_framing_risks: Keep publications, research, and PhD language out of positive positioning unless directly required by the target role.
Do not imply research ownership, model invention, or academic specialization from applied platform work.
domain_or_region_risks: Do not claim native-language requirements; none are evidenced in the deep-analysis summary.
Do not overstate remote-only preference even though remote-first appears often; present distributed collaboration as a working style, not a core identity.
Do not overfit the CV to one vertical because domain distribution is mixed and shallow: fintech 3/34, healthcare 3/34, consulting 2/34, enterprise SaaS 2/34.
citations: Unsafe claims sample includes formal people-management org ownership, strategic hiring ownership, deep healthcare ownership, observability ownership at scale, deep Oracle specialization, deep consulting background, and unsupported stack ownership.
Native language requirements: none. Domain distribution: fintech 3/34, healthcare 3/34, consulting 2/34, enterprise_saas 2/34.

overall_assessment.one_sentence_verdict: Strong production AI platform evidence with Commander-4/Joyia directly matching the category's core signals—platform architecture, evaluation harnesses, RAG pipelines, and reliability—while mentoring and player-coach evidence aligns with the senior IC profile; main gaps are headline framing and surfacing latency/scale metrics more explicitly.
overall_assessment.uncertainty_note: Representation is proxy-derived from role_metadata.json; headline adjustment from 'Technical Architect' to 'Staff AI Engineer' framing is category-critical but not yet reflected in curated master CV.
citations: Platform design 34/34 (100%); candidate evidence shows Commander-4 platform serving 2,000 users across 42 plugins — data/master-cv/roles/01_seven_one_entertainment.md:28
Evaluation_quality 15/34 (44.1%); candidate has MRR@k, NDCG@k retrieval evaluation — data/master-cv/projects/commander4_skills.json:22

weighted_score_explanation: candidate_evidence_coverage_score (8.5 × 0.30 = 2.55): Commander-4 provides direct evidence for platform design, evaluation harnesses, RAG pipelines, hybrid search, semantic caching, and guardrails—matching the category's table-stakes and differentiators. Minor gap in explicit agentic workflow depth.

evidence_curation_completeness_score (7.5 × 0.15 = 1.125): Core AI platform work is well-curated with detailed technical implementation; upstream STAR records contain additional scalability and cross-functional evidence not yet promoted.

master_cv_representation_quality_score (7.0 × 0.25 = 1.75): AI platform work prominently featured but headline uses 'Technical Architect' instead of category-aligned 'Staff AI Engineer'; reliability and latency metrics underrepresented in priority bullets.

ai_architecture_fit_score (9.0 × 0.15 = 1.35): Enterprise AI platform with 2,000 users, hybrid retrieval (BM25+RRF), LLM-as-judge reranking, evaluation harness (MRR/NDCG), semantic caching, guardrails, and MCP tools—excellent category fit.

leadership_scope_fit_score (8.0 × 0.10 = 0.80): Player-coach evidence with 10+ engineers mentored and 3 promoted; hiring evidence exists but appropriately understated; not overclaiming formal management.

impact_proof_strength_score (7.5 × 0.05 = 0.375): Quantified outcomes (2,000 users, 42 plugins, 75% incident reduction, 3 years zero downtime) but latency/cost optimization metrics could be more explicit.

strongest_supported_signals.signal: Production AI platform architecture at enterprise scale
strongest_supported_signals.why_it_matters_for_category: Platform design is the clearest category anchor (34/34, 100%); Commander-4 serving 2,000 users across 42 plugins directly matches the primary hiring signal.
strongest_supported_signals.citation: Platform design 34/34 (100%); valued evidence type architecture 20/20 deep-analysis samples.

strongest_supported_signals.signal: Retrieval evaluation with quality metrics (MRR@k, NDCG@k)
strongest_supported_signals.why_it_matters_for_category: Evaluation is the strongest AI-specific differentiator (15/34, 44.1%); explicit MRR/NDCG implementation with unit tests proves mature applied AI engineering.
strongest_supported_signals.citation: Evaluation_quality 15/34 (44.1%); valued evidence type evaluation 15/20 deep-analysis samples.

strongest_supported_signals.signal: RAG pipeline with hybrid search (BM25 + RRF + semantic)
strongest_supported_signals.why_it_matters_for_category: RAG is a meaningful differentiator (10/34, 29.4%); custom BM25 implementation with RRF fusion and LLM-as-judge reranking shows production retrieval depth.
Guardrails supports applied AI stories (5/34, 14.7%); per-silo guardrail injection with access-control-aware behavior shows production safety patterns.
strongest_supported_signals.citation: Guardrails_governance 5/34 (14.7%); pain points emphasize production controls and safe AI behavior.

strongest_supported_signals.why_it_matters_for_category: Reliability is table-stakes (18/20 valued evidence); 75% incident reduction and 3 years zero downtime proves stable production operation.
strongest_supported_signals.citation: Valued evidence type reliability 18/20; success metrics include reliability, resilience, and high availability.

strongest_supported_signals.signal: Mentoring and technical guidance without formal management
strongest_supported_signals.why_it_matters_for_category: Mentoring is table-stakes for player-coach profile (20/34, 58.8%); 10+ engineers mentored with 3 promoted to lead positions matches the role scope.
Collaboration (29/34, 85.3%) and communication (23/34, 67.6%) are the most repeated soft-skill signals; stakeholder alignment and business education evidence supports this.

gap_analysis_top.signal: Explicit latency/throughput metrics for AI serving
gap_analysis_top.why_it_matters: Pain points repeatedly emphasize low latency, high throughput, and maintainable scale (valued evidence types scale 14/20, cost 11/20); success metrics include latency optimization.
gap_analysis_top.current_state: Semantic caching latencies mentioned (~2ms L1, ~200ms L2) but not prominently surfaced; scaling patterns exist in legacy modernization but not connected to AI-specific throughput.
gap_analysis_top.safe_interpretation: Can claim latency-aware design for semantic caching and general platform scalability; cannot claim specific p99 latency targets or throughput benchmarks for LLM serving without additional evidence.
gap_analysis_top.recommended_action: Surface the 2ms/200ms cache latency metrics more prominently in role bullets; add any available throughput metrics from Commander-4 production.
gap_analysis_top.citation: Valued evidence types scale 14/20, cost 11/20; success metrics include scalability, performance, latency optimization.

gap_analysis_top.why_it_matters: Agents/orchestration appears at 9/34 (26.5%); differentiator when tied to production controls and automation outcomes.
gap_analysis_top.current_state: MCP tool design (5 server tools) curated but not framed as agentic orchestration; structured outputs and tool-calling architecture exist but 'agent' language absent.
gap_analysis_top.safe_interpretation: Can claim tool-calling architecture and MCP integrations for external system access; frame as 'workflow orchestration' rather than autonomous agents unless deeper evidence exists.
gap_analysis_top.recommended_action: Reframe MCP tool design and structured outputs as 'workflow orchestration' in experience bullets; clarify if any autonomous agent behavior exists in Commander-4.

gap_analysis_top.signal: Scalability and performance optimization for ML workloads
gap_analysis_top.why_it_matters: The category values scaling AI-enabled services under latency/throughput/reliability constraints as a key achievement archetype (rank 4).
gap_analysis_top.current_state: Upstream STAR records mention scalability optimization but AI-specific scaling patterns not yet promoted to curated store; Lambda/ECS auto-scaling mentioned for backend, not explicitly for ML inference.
gap_analysis_top.safe_interpretation: Can claim platform-level scaling experience; ML-specific throughput optimization requires curation from upstream or new evidence.
gap_analysis_top.recommended_action: Review upstream STAR records (docs/archive/knowledge-base.md:360, :546) for any ML-specific scaling evidence; curate if found.
gap_analysis_top.citation: Success metrics include scalability, performance, resilience; valued evidence types reliability 18/20, scale 14/20, cost 11/20.

gap_analysis_top.why_it_matters: Title families concentrate in staff_engineer (20/34) and principal_engineer (13/34); headline must reflect senior IC scope with multi-team architecture influence.
gap_analysis_top.current_state: Current headline is 'Technical Architect / AI Platform Architect' which signals architecture scope but not Staff/Principal IC framing expected by this category.
gap_analysis_top.safe_interpretation: Evidence supports Staff AI Engineer positioning (11 years experience, platform lead, multi-team influence); headline adjustment is representation issue not evidence gap.
gap_analysis_top.recommended_action: Update headline to 'Staff AI Engineer' or 'Principal AI Engineer' framing with 'Platform Architecture' qualifier; current 'Technical Architect' is adjacent but not category-aligned.

gap_analysis_top.signal: Fine-tuning, RLHF, or custom embedding training
gap_analysis_top.why_it_matters: Fine-tuning appears at 4/34 (11.8%) in the category; candidate explicitly lists these as do_not_claim.
gap_analysis_top.current_state: Explicitly marked as do_not_claim: fine-tuning pipeline, custom embedding training, RLHF.
gap_analysis_top.safe_interpretation: Do not claim any fine-tuning, RLHF, or custom embedding work; category does not require these (low frequency at 11.8%).
gap_analysis_top.recommended_action: Maintain explicit exclusion in skills; this is not a material gap since fine-tuning is optional for the category.
gap_analysis_top.citation: Fine_tuning 4/34 (11.8%); marked do_not_claim in candidate evidence.

gap_analysis_top.signal: Cost optimization metrics for AI inference
gap_analysis_top.why_it_matters: Cost evidence is valued (11/20 deep-analysis samples); semantic caching implies cost reduction but no explicit metrics surfaced.
gap_analysis_top.current_state: Two-tier semantic caching designed to reduce redundant LLM calls; Lantern project mentions '~40% reduction in redundant API spend' but this is personal project not enterprise.
gap_analysis_top.safe_interpretation: Can claim cost-aware design patterns; specific percentage claims require enterprise context confirmation.
gap_analysis_top.recommended_action: If Commander-4 has cost reduction metrics from semantic caching, surface them; otherwise frame as 'cost-optimized caching design'.
gap_analysis_top.citation: Valued evidence type cost 11/20; semantic caching reduces redundant API calls.
